text.title: Late-Invoice Dunning Sequence Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Late-Invoice Dunning Sequence application must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Reminders: Day 0 (invoice), Day +7 (gentle reminder), Day +14 (firm), Day +21 (late fee notice), Day +30 (work-stoppage notice). Cadence is automatic, not opportunistic.
rule.rationale: Manual dunning collapses under awkwardness; automated cadence applies neutral pressure consistently.

rule.statement: Each stage has a written template. Templates are professional, brief, escalating. No emotional language.
rule.rationale: Templates remove drafting friction at peak awkwardness; professional tone protects relationship.

rule.statement: Late fee = 1.5%/month OR statutory rate, whichever is greater. Trigger requires a clause in the contract OR jurisdiction's statutory default.
rule.rationale: Late fees without contractual basis are unenforceable; clause is the lever.

rule.statement: Work stoppage triggered at Day +30 unpaid (or contractual minimum). Stop is in writing, professional, references contract.
rule.rationale: Free continued work is the silent margin killer; explicit stop is the only way to break the pattern.

rule.statement: Day +90 unpaid AND ≥$1k → small-claims (cheap, fast) OR collections (15-30% commission). Decision based on amount + jurisdiction.
rule.rationale: Long-tail receivables compound; explicit gate prevents perpetual chase.

rule.statement: When preconditions in `Applies If` fail OR any item in `Skip If` triggers, do not run this methodology. Document the skip rationale in the artefact's parent log.
rule.rationale: Misapplied methodologies are worse than no methodology — they consume review cycles and produce false confidence.
